agent_output:
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Timezone-blind cutoff check uses server UTC time instead of customer local time, causing wrong accept/reject decisions for every non-UTC customer."
  },

  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "getHours() returns server-UTC hours, not customer local time",
      "risk": "New York customer placing an order at 10:00 AM EST (15:00 UTC) is rejected because 15 >= 14. London BST customer ordering at 14:30 local (13:30 UTC) is incorrectly accepted. Every timezone-offset customer gets a wrong cutoff.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "isSameDayDeliveryAvailable — `orderTime.getHours()` call",
      "reproduction": "1. Server runs in UTC. 2. New York customer orders at 10:00 AM ET (EST, UTC-5) → Date is 15:00 UTC → getHours() returns 15 → rejected despite being 4 hours before their local cutoff. 3. London customer in BST orders at 14:30 local (13:30 UTC) → getHours() returns 13 → accepted despite being past their local cutoff.",
      "fix": "Accept a customer timezone parameter (IANA zone string) and evaluate the cutoff in that timezone:\n\n```typescript\nfunction isSameDayDeliveryAvailable(\n  orderTime: Date,\n  customerTimeZone: string // e.g. 'Europe/London', 'America/New_York'\n): boolean {\n  const cutoffHour = 14;\n  const localHour = new Intl.DateTimeFormat('en-US', {\n    hour: 'numeric',\n    hour12: false,\n    timeZone: customerTimeZone,\n  }).formatToParts(orderTime)\n    .find(p => p.type === 'hour')!;\n  return parseInt(localHour.value, 10) < cutoffHour;\n}\n```\nThis uses the built-in `Intl` API (no extra deps) and correctly handles DST transitions automatically via IANA zone names.",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Same-day delivery eligibility for all customers outside UTC+0 winter"
    },
    {
      "title": "DST transition boundary produces silent off-by-one for hours around the cutoff",
      "risk": "On DST changeover days (e.g. last Sunday of March in UK, second Sunday of March in US), clocks spring forward or fall back. A customer ordering at 13:50 local time on spring-forward day could map to a different UTC hour than expected, flipping the accept/reject decision. The current code has zero DST awareness.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "isSameDayDeliveryAvailable — no timezone or DST handling",
      "reproduction": "UK clocks spring forward at 01:00 on last Sunday of March. Customer orders at 13:50 BST. If the Date object was constructed from a naive hour (e.g., form submission parsed without timezone), the UTC value may be 12:50 or 13:50 depending on how the input was parsed — the function cannot distinguish these cases.",
      "fix": "Covered by the IANA timezone fix above. Intl.DateTimeFormat with a named timezone handles DST transitions correctly by definition — no manual offset math needed.",
      "time_to_fix": "Included in fix above",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Correctness on DST transition days (~4 days/year, 2 per supported timezone)"
    },
    {
      "title": "No per-region cutoff configuration as platform expands",
      "risk": "Hard-coded `14` assumes the same cutoff hour applies to London and New York. Logistics, warehouse locations, and carrier pickup schedules differ by region. Shipping the current design locks in a single cutoff that will need an emergency change when New York operations have a different cutoff (e.g., 12:00 due to later last-mile pickup).",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "isSameDayDeliveryAvailable — `const cutoffHour = 14`",
      "reproduction": "New York warehouse has a 12:00 local carrier pickup. Customer orders at 13:00 ET. Function (even if timezone-fixed) says eligible. Package misses the carrier → delivery is next-day, not same-day → broken customer promise.",
      "fix": "Make cutoff configurable per region:\n\n```typescript\nconst CUTOFF_BY_ZONE: Record<string, number> = {\n  'Europe/London': 14,\n  'America/New_York': 14, // adjust when ops confirms\n};\n\nfunction isSameDayDeliveryAvailable(\n  orderTime: Date,\n  customerTimeZone: string\n): boolean {\n  const cutoffHour = CUTOFF_BY_ZONE[customerTimeZone];\n  if (cutoffHour === undefined) return false; // fail closed for unknown zones\n  // ... timezone-aware hour check\n}\n```",
      "time_to_fix": "15 minutes on top of the timezone fix",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Multi-region expansion to New York"
    }
  ],

  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "Function fails open for unknown/unsupported timezones",
      "description": "If a customer's timezone is not provided or is invalid, the current function still returns true/false based on UTC. It should fail closed (return false) for unsupported regions to avoid promising same-day delivery that cannot be fulfilled.",
      "location": "isSameDayDeliveryAvailable return value"
    },
    {
      "title": "No minute-level precision near cutoff boundary",
      "description": "Only checking the hour means an order at 13:59:59 is accepted but an order at 14:00:00 is rejected. This is technically correct per spec, but consider whether the cutoff should include a processing buffer (e.g., 13:45) to account for payment processing latency between 'order placed' and 'order confirmed'.",
      "location": "isSameDayDeliveryAvailable — hour-only comparison"
    }
  ],

  "suggestions": [
    "Add unit tests for: UTC midnight, 13:59 local, 14:00 local, 14:01 local, DST spring-forward day, DST fall-back day, and each supported timezone.",
    "Log rejected same-day requests with both UTC and resolved local time for debugging timezone mismatches in production.",
    "Consider using a lightweight date library (date-fns-tz or Temporal API if targeting Node 20+) instead of Intl.DateTimeFormat.formatToParts for cleaner ergonomics."
  ],

  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "Any deployment of this function as-is will immediately produce incorrect same-day eligibility decisions for New York customers (off by 5 hours) and London BST customers (off by 1 hour in summer).",
    "rollback_capability": "Pure function with no state — rollback is trivial by reverting the code.",
    "backward_compatibility": "Fix changes function signature (adds customerTimeZone parameter), so all call sites must be updated. This is a breaking change to the internal API.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "No monitoring mentioned. Recommend tracking same-day eligibility accept/reject rates per timezone to catch regressions.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": false,
      "data_pipeline": false,
      "config_changes": false,
      "details": ["Internal function signature changes from (Date) to (Date, string) — all callers need updating"]
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Revert to previous version. No persistent state affected."
  },

  "escalations": [],

  "confidence": {
    "level": 97,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Timezone miscalculation is deterministic and provable — getHours() on a UTC server cannot return customer-local hours",
      "DST handling gap follows directly from the same root cause"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Whether the business actually requires different cutoffs per region (assumed likely given different logistics)"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The orderTime Date object represents the actual moment of order placement (not already converted to customer local time before being passed in)",
      "The spec's '14:00 local time' means customer-local, not server-local"
    ]
  }
}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this function for production risks.

The function checks if same-day delivery is available based on a 14:00 cutoff, but it uses `getHours()` which returns hours in the server's local timezone (UTC since the server runs in UTC). The customer's local time is never considered.

Key issues:
1. Timezone blindness - `getHours()` uses server time (UTC), not customer local time. A London customer ordering at 14:30 BST (13:30 UTC) would be allowed, but a New York customer ordering at 10:00 EST (15:00 UTC) would be rejected.


2. DST transitions aren't handled, and the function has no way to know which timezone to evaluate against.

These are straightforward code-level fixes with established solutions like passing timezone information and using proper date libraries.
